EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of Monday 18 February, a male Russian tourist, 60 years old, was drowned off of Obyan Beach on southern Saipan after he ventured outside the reef while snorkeling and was carried out in strong rip currents. At about 2140 GST, his body was located by the crew of a Coast guard helicopter, and subsequently recovered.

A 61-year-old male Saipan resident was swept out over the reef while snorkeling at Obyan Beach on southern Saipan about 1645 GST the afternoon of Tuesday, 26 February. He was reported missing at 1930 GST. His body has not been recovered, and he is presumed drowned.
Hazardous Surf and Strong Rip Currents Caused a number of deaths across|Guam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On December 20th by-standers pulled a 67 year old male Japanese tourist pulled from Tumon Bay around 11 am. It happened in Tumon between the Outrigger and Nana's Cafe. The man was  unconscious and unresponsive. CPR was immediately started and it continued as the man was rushed to GMH. The man remained in critical condition for a few days. On December 24th was pronounced dead at GMH.  ||A High Surf Advisory was in effect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 7, 2013 A lightning strike caused the shutdown of the National Telecommunication Authority (NTA) power line system at the Rita substation, disrupting television service. According to NTA MHTV Transmission Supervisor Rayner Peter Jr., the power system and backup generator were damaged.This also disrupted television, internet and cellular phone service in Majuro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On July 7, 2013 A lightning strike caused the shutdown of the National Telecommunication Authority (NTA) power line system at the Rita substation, disrupting television service. According to NTA MHTV Transmission Supervisor Rayner Peter Jr., the power system and backup generator were damaged.This also disrupted television, internet and cellular phone service in Majuro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Haiyan crossed Micronesia from November 3rd to the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of November 1st scatterometer data detected a circulation just south of Kosrae. This circulation moved toward the west-northwest and intensified over the next few days. The strengthening was such that on the evening of November 3 the National Weather Service Office in Guam started issuing Special Weather Statements on it. At that time it was still a tropical disturbance and was located near 6N156E or 95 miles west of Pohnpei. Further intensification resulted in the disturbance reaching tropical depression strength. On the night of November 3rd the first advisory was issued for Tropical Depression 31W when it was located near 6N155E. Even though it was only a depression it was expected to quickly intensify. This expectation was borne out as the depression became a tropical storm on the afternoon of the 4th and a typhoon by the afternoon of the 5th. Haiyan, as the system was named was located near 6N145E as it attained typhoon strength. This intensification did not cease as Haiyan became a super typhoon on the afternoon of the 6th. The location of Haiyan at that time was near 8N139E or 300 miles east of Koror Palau. Haiayn had maximum sustained winds of 150 mph with gust estimated to be near 175 mph. On the evening|of the 6th it passed very close to the atoll of Ngulu. The storm surge generated by |Haiyan overspread the atoll. The atoll was inhabited by only six people, none of these|were evacuated. Despite the devastation caused by the storm surge, all of these people survived.|     As Haiyan continued its trek westward its eye passed over Kayangel on the morning of the 7th. The population of this atoll at that time was around 100 people with 30 of them being evacuated to Koror before the typhoon arrived. Most of these evacuees were elderly, women and children. 69 people remained on Kayangel as the eye of Haiyan passed over it. The super typhoon's destructive winds severely damaged|the atoll's only school and clinic. In addtion, roofs of most of the houses on Kayangel were blown off. Many trees were toppled by the strong winds. Power lines as well as communication lines were totally damaged. Storm surge associated with Haiyan pushed sea water inland, contaminating planting fields and the atoll's fresh water lens. The spoiling of the lens means that the people of Kayangel will have to import fresh water|from Palau. Eventually it is hoped that a water desalinization plant will be installed to produce drinking water. |     It could cost the Palauan government $10 million to rebuild power and water infrastructure, as well schools and homes. Despite the intensity of the typhoon and the damage it incurred, no deaths were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing tropical cyclone and a monsoon surge brought heavy rain and gusty |winds to the Marianas. On 10 October the disturbance that would become Tropical Depression 25W passes close to Guam. As it passed it generated heavy showers and gusty winds over the area. Tropical Depression 25W forms 145 miles west of Guam on the morning of the 11th. By the afternoon 25W became Tropical Storm Wipha 195 miles west of Guam. Increased monsoon winds associated with the tropical storm was observed on Guam. The tropical storm and a associated surge in the monsoon continued gusty wind across the Marianas on the 12th and 13th October. The swell associated with the surge produced dangerous surf on west and north facing reefs of the Marianas. The winds, surf and swell damaged an old dock at Agat Mariana on Guam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tropical Disturbance that would become Tropical Depression 25W passes close to Guam. There were periods of heavy rain during the day across Guam. Between|three to five inches of rain fell across the island. Some reports from across the |island:|		4.18 inches of rain was recorded at National Weather Service Office,|		3.29 inches at Apra Harbor Automated Weather Station,|		3.35 inches at Inarajan Automated Weather Station,|		5.26 inches at Merizo Automated Weather Station,|		4.35 inches at Windward Automated Weather Station, |An Urban and Small Stream Advisory was issued during the day for the rain.

By the afternoon of the 16th the disturbance had become Tropical Depression 26W and was located around|75 miles southeast of Guam. The depression passed south of Guam during that afternoon. A Tropical Storm Watch was issued for Guam, Rota, Tinian, and Saipan. During the early morning of October 17th TD 26W becomes Tropical Storm Francisco 175 miles southwest of Guam, by 2 pm it was a typhoon 205 miles southwest of Guam. Typhoon Francisco passed 140 miles west of Guam at 7 am on the 18th of October.|Typhoon Francisco was located 200 miles west of the radar at 11 am. The diameter of the eye was estimated to be around 29 miles.  Gusty winds were observed across the Marianas. On the morning of the October 19th Typhoon Francisco became a |Super Typhoon 330 miles northwest of Guam. Outer rain bands associated with Francisco brought a few wind gust over the Marianas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression 25W forms 145 miles west of Guam on the morning of the 11th.|By that afternoon 25W became Tropical Storm Wipha 195 miles west of Guam.|Increased monsoon winds associated with the tropical storm was observed on Guam.|A winds gust of 35 mph was observed at the NWS office, 49 mph AT Andersen AFB, 40 mph at Saipan, and 39 mph at Rota. ||The winds across the Western Pacific associated with the monsoon and Tropical|Storm Wipha generated large swells. The strong winds and swells generated by the monsoon surge caused damage to the 25 year old Dock D at Agat Marina in Guam. About 65 percent of the dock was destroyed by seawater surging through the marina. Several million dollars is the estimate of the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fisherman drowns on Guam on the 20th of November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the night of November 20th the Guam Fire Department received a phone call about a missing fisherman. The call came in around 1025 pm and originated from two men who managed to swim to shore. Three men had gone spear fishing that night in the Asan Channel not far from the War in the Pacific Park. One of the trio was swept out to sea by rough waves. His two companions swam to shore after this wave hit them and immediately called for help. The Fire Department and Coast Guard conducted a three hour search which unfortunately recovered the man���s body. Data from the Ritidian Point buoy located not too far from the area showed that a brief period of higher surf and strong rips currents could have been possible around the time of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hazardous Surf and Strong Rip Currents Caused a number of deaths across|Guam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On December 20th 2013 around 11:12 am three male individuals were attempting to exit the mouth of the Hagatna Boat Basin in a Livingston double hulled boat, when the boat was struck by a large wave causing the boat to be flipped backwards tossing all three occupants into the water.  None of the three occupants were utilizing any type of personal flotation devices at the time. A para-sailing boat from the Ocean Jet Club was heading back into the channel at the time and picked up two survivors, not realizing that a third person was unaccounted for.  Fire rescue and marine patrol arrived shortly.  Fire Rescue located the victim face down unresponsive and lifeless in the water. Resuscitation attempts were underway while en-route to Naval Hospital where he was pronounced dead. The victim was a 50 year old male. Of the other two occupants of the vessel,one male was transported to GMH and was responsive, the other male was not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second Hazardous Surf and Strong Rip Current Event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At around 12:00pm, Wednesday, December 25, 2013, Katwin Dois (37) and Edward Sotam (40) arrived at the Jonestown Overlook in Tamuning with two other relatives|where they started drinking beer and vodka at the fishing area. Around 3:40 pm, Katwin fell off the right side of the cliff line onto a rock platform, rendering Katwin unconscious. The waves of the ocean swept him out to sea. Anso Sotam and Katner Dois jumped into the ocean towards the left side of the cliff line to help Katwin. A few minutes later, Edward Sotam jumped into the ocean by the white wash area, where the current was strong and Edward Sotam could not swim out from the white wash area, where the waves slammed Edward Sotam against the cliff line, rendering him (Edward) unconscious. |     Anso and Katner were able to retrieve Katwin���s body, however they could not bring Katwin���s body to shore, due to the current was too strong for them, so they treaded water in the deeper portion of the ocean. Shortly thereafter, Anso swam to shore to call help. |     The GPD Harbor Patrol Unit, The GFD���s Anchor #1, Rescue #1, Rescue #2, Rescue #3, Engine #3 and Medic #5 were activated and later arrived at scene. The GFD���s units were able to locate Edward Sotam and Edward Sotam was transported to the Navy Regional Medical Center (NRMC) for further medical treatment, where Edward Sotam was later pronounced dead at 5:22 pm. |     At around 5:30 p.m., this date, GFD���s Units were able to locate the body of Katwin, which was later taken to Hagatna Boat Basin by the GPD Harbor Patrol Unit, where Katwin���s body was transported by GFD medic units to GMH for further medical treatment, where Katwin was pronounced dead on arrival at 6:30 p.m., this date. |     |A High Surf Advisory was in effect.

During the second half of the month it became more active and was either over or north of Guam. ||By the morning of the 16th a circulation on the monsoon trough was located east|of Guam. This situation persisted the next few days. A surge in the monsoon |associated with the developing circulation generated gusty winds and heavy|showers over Guam from the 19th through the 22nd of the month.  ||The monsoon trough with an embedded circulation was located over the Marianas on the morning of the 19th||On the morning of the 21st the Circulation became TD 19W near 20N146E. 19W became Tropical Storm Pabuk on the night of the 21st.||With the monsoon near Guam rainfall was on and off for a few days. From the 14th to 18th 2.40 inches of rain fell. On the 19th 10.2 inches of rain fell, with an additional 8.21 inches on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the National Weather Service office Winds gusted to 41 mph on the afternoon of the 19th. Andersen Air Force Base located in northern Guam recorded a wind gust to 44 mph during the afternoon. Numerous wind gusts across Guam downed some trees. Sporadic power outages were from north to south. The winds caused scattered power outages in a few locations. These outages affected some schools in Merizo and Agana Heights. One tree that was knocked down along Marine Corps Drive in East Hagatna blocked two southbound lanes during the afternoon of the 19th.

On this day 10.2 inches of rain |was reported at the NWS Office in Barrigada, Guam.The heavy rain forced one family|out of their home. Also locations in upper Tumon, Hagatna and Harmon experienced Urban Flooding. | Yona Mayor Ken Joe Ada said Pulantat Road was closed because of flooding, and the resident of one home there had to be evacuated, a car there was also submerged.| The flooding in the Pulantat area of Yona caused at least two families to leave their homes. Pulantat Road was covered by several feet of water.  || Flooding in Barrigada caused three families to be evacuated from their homes. ||	The weather caused 13 Guam bound flights to divert to Saipan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain showers again caused Flash Flooding across portions of Guam. 8.21 inches |of rain was reported at the NWS office in Barrigada on this date.| Water entered the campus of Simon Sanchez High School any way it could this morning. ���We had a lot of classrooms that were leaking because the water was coming through the vents, through the air con ducts, or through leaks on the roof,���  Simon Sanchez High School Assistant Principal Melvin Finona told Pacific News Center. ���The water level was four, five inches in some areas.��� The rains also came in through the doors of the campus. Flooding in the science wing was so bad that students had to be evacuated to the cafeteria.  According to Finona conditions were okay as students reported to class this morning. Heavy rains however became a problem by 9 a.m., prompting the school to dismiss students at 11:30 a.m.. Some areas of Pulantat were impassable because of flooding. 35 families are receiving assistance from the American Red Cross after heavy rains over the past week damaged their homes. Disaster Services Director Dave Peredo says three or four of those families have been temporarily relocated to hotels while they make their homes livable again.| The Red Cross is providing funding for flood victims to purchase food, clothing and other emergency needs. Clean up kits are	also being given out to help residents clean up the flood water. With more calls for help coming in today, Peredo expects that the| 	Red Cross may end up helping more families victimized by the recent rains.
